title: Carlsen vs Caruana — 2018 Tiebreak Game 1
description: Carlsen draws first blood in the rapid playoff, grinding out a tricky rook endgame after Caruana lets the White king escape its cage. Annotated by GM Sam Shankland.

conceptChecks.prompt: Why does the annotator like Carlsen's 4.e4 ("!?")?

options.text: It transposes to his favorite Rossolimo-type structures but a tempo up, an excellent practical choice where White can never be worse.
options.explanation: Correct. The note: "aiming just to get his typical Rossolimo positions a tempo up... the extra tempo means White can never be worse. Objectively Black must be fine, but practically, this was excellent preparation."

options.text: It wins the e5-pawn by force.
options.explanation: No — it's a strategic transposition, not a material grab.

options.text: It is objectively the only move that fights for an edge.
options.explanation: The annotator says 'objectively Black must be fine'; the value is practical, not objective superiority.

conceptChecks.hint: Think about which familiar structures Carlsen is steering toward.

conceptChecks.prompt: The annotator marks 8.d4 with a "!". What is his assessment?

options.text: It's energetic and strong — Carlsen is playing with a lot of force.
options.explanation: Correct. The note: "Energetic and strong... Magnus is playing with a lot of force," and a move later praises him for 'not fearing the loss of a pawn.'

options.text: It's an unsound pawn sacrifice the annotator criticizes.
options.explanation: The opposite — he praises it as strong and admires White's willingness to give up a pawn.

options.text: It bails out into an immediate draw.
options.explanation: No; the annotator stresses White's forceful play for the advantage.

conceptChecks.hint: Is this a cautious move or a forceful one?

conceptChecks.prompt: What is the point of Caruana's 16...Rb3 ("!")?

options.text: Although the c-pawn will eventually fall, Black compromises White's pawn structure before it's captured.
options.explanation: Correct. The note: "The c-pawn will eventually fall, but Black can compromise White's structure before it is captured."

options.text: It permanently defends and saves the extra c-pawn.
options.explanation: No — the annotator says the c-pawn 'will eventually fall'; the idea is to wreck White's structure first.

options.text: It wins material with a fork on b2.
options.explanation: No fork or material win is mentioned.

conceptChecks.hint: What does Black get in return for the c-pawn?

conceptChecks.prompt: The annotator gives 19...Nb5 a "?". Why is it a mistake?

options.text: Chasing activity here makes Black's position fall apart; the passive 19...Nb7 was the prudent necessity.
options.explanation: Correct. The note: "Activity is always appealing and often correct, but now the position falls apart," while 19...Nb7 'may have been prudent... not falling apart just yet.'

options.text: It simply hangs the knight for nothing.
options.explanation: Not the reasoning — the whole position collapses tactically, whereas 19...Nb7 holds on.

options.text: It's actually the most accurate, equalizing move.
options.explanation: No; the annotator marks it a clear mistake versus the prudent 19...Nb7.

conceptChecks.hint: Activity is tempting, but what happens to the rest of the position?

conceptChecks.prompt: Carlsen plays 24.Bxe6+ ("?"), which the annotator calls "imprecise." What did White miss?

options.text: 24.Rxd4!, when the lethal pin wins — though it required spotting a very difficult follow-up.
options.explanation: Correct. The note: "24.Rxd4! The pin is lethal. White has to spot a very difficult move, but then he wins," with the stunning 25.Kh1!!
No — the annotator's improvement is 24.Rxd4! exploiting the pin, not Bxc4.

options.text: A forced queen-mate against the black king.
options.explanation: The queens were traded back on move 13; the win is via the lethal pin, not a queen mate.

conceptChecks.hint: Which white rook could have exploited the pinned knight?

conceptChecks.prompt: The annotator condemns 34...Rc3 ("?"). What principle did Black violate?

options.text: He let White's king into the game; 34...c3 followed by ...Ra2 would have drawn by keeping the king passive.
options.explanation: Correct. The note: "Black absolutely cannot let the White king enter the game," and the line 34...c3 ...Ra2 "draws easily. The king is simply too passive."

options.text: He should have queened the c-pawn by force instead.
options.explanation: No — the drawing method was to restrain White's king (34...c3, ...Ra2), not a winning pawn race.

options.text: It hangs the rook on c3.
options.explanation: No; the flaw is strategic — allowing 35.Kg2 ('Freedom!'), not a material blunder.

conceptChecks.hint: What does the very next move, 35.Kg2 ('Freedom!'), tell you?
